titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: Catalina Chatter. The Summer Season's Premature End. Duyckinck's Thousand Fish

div.p: Slowly and before its time the island is being depopulated. Yesterday a crowd came over on the steamer at 1 oclock, but returned at 3 oclock. September is the most delightful month here, but habit and school terms are unreasoning tyrants.

Capt. "Sandy" McDonald is telling the boys about a wonderful dog that he rescued last week, who had managed to follow his mistress from Long Beach to the isthmus after she had left him on the mainland, disconsolate and alone.
Herbert Gregg entertained Misses Gertrude and Mae McCrea, Frances, Dorothy and Alice Groff and Edith Furrey, Messrs. Burnham and Harry Polley at a candy pull Saturday afternoon.

Mr. Duyckinck, who holds the record for the number of fish caught this season on rod and reel, is now bending all his efforts to catch the thirty-three fish necessary to make his total 1000 fish. Saturday he was out with Mexican Joe and caught fifteen yellowtail. The fish were found at the isthmus and in great numbers, but the limited time that he had to spend there did not allow him to get any more fish.

The church on the hill is to be enlarged during the winter. The additional room will be fitted out and used as a meeting and club room for those who stay here all winter. Magazines will be on the tables. Over $550 has been raised for the purpose.
Dr. C. W. Bryson and J. Todd Cook secured seven goat heads the other day. N. Howard, Mr. and Mrs. L. C. Craig and H. M. Corlett also secured four fine heads.

The yacht San Diego returned from San Pedro yesterday. This afternoon the San Diego will leave on an exploration trip to San Nicolas. A search will be made for the Cave of the Lost Woman, who was marooned on the island years ago. The party will consist of E. L. Doran, Sid Smith and C. F. Holder.
The Hotel Pasadena has closed for the season.
Miss Craig of Pasadena is the guest of Mr. and Mrs. J. B. Banning.
Mr. and Mrs. Parry Gavin have returned to their home in Pasadena after spending the summer here.
